title: Asset Risk Scoring (CIM)
spl:
index=security tag=vulnerability status=open
| eval w=case(severity="critical",10, severity="high",7, severity="medium",4, 1)
| stats sum(w) as risk_score, dc(cve) as cve_count by dest
| sort -risk_score
| head 100
cimSpl: | tstats `summariesonly` dc(Vulnerabilities.cve) as cves from datamodel=Vulnerabilities.Vulnerabilities where Vulnerabilities.status="open" by Vulnerabilities.dest, Vulnerabilities.severity
description: Aggregate open vulns per `dest` weighted by CVSS for top-risk hosts.
value: Aggregate open vulns per `dest` weighted by CVSS for top-risk hosts.
implementation: Blend with threat intel for exploited-in-wild CVEs (2√ó weight).
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Vulnerabilities CIM.
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: `dest`, `severity`, `cve`.
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
Blend with threat intel for exploited-in-wild CVEs (2√ó weight).

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=security tag=vulnerability status=open
| eval w=case(severity="critical",10, severity="high",7, severity="medium",4, 1)
| stats sum(w) as risk_score, dc(cve) as cve_count by dest
| sort -risk_score
| head 100
```

Understanding this SPL

**Asset Risk Scoring (CIM)** ‚Äî Aggregate open vulns per `dest` weighted by CVSS for top-risk hosts.

Documented **Data sources**: `dest`, `severity`, `cve`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Vulnerabilities CIM. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: security.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=security, tags. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **w** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by dest** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.
‚Ä¢ Limits the number of rows with `head`.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` dc(Vulnerabilities.cve) as cves from datamodel=Vulnerabilities.Vulnerabilities where Vulnerabilities.status="open" by Vulnerabilities.dest, Vulnerabilities.severity
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Asset Risk Scoring (CIM)** ‚Äî Aggregate open vulns per `dest` weighted by CVSS for top-risk hosts.

Documented **Data sources**: `dest`, `severity`, `cve`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Vulnerabilities CIM. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Vulnerabilities.Vulnerabilities` ‚Äî enable acceleration for that model.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (top risk assets), Bubble chart.
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern ‚Äî correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
